coredata.dc:title: Efficient and economically affordable TiO2 nanotube-based ternary photocatalysts for CO2 conversion boosted by NiO nanoparticles and carbon quantum dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Fawzi, Tarek
Huang, Pei-Chen
Kim, Jinwook
Hung, Wei-Hsuan
Lin, Wei-Chun
Lee, Hyeonseok
coredata.dc:description:
Photocatalytic CO2 conversion, in general, requires the incorporation of expensive and noble materials to achieve a highly efficient conversion rate. Herein, the TNT/NiO/CQD ternary nanocomposites without any expensive materials and material modification are fabricated for the demonstration of efficient and economically affordable photocatalytic CO2 conversion, to our best knowledge, for the first time. The TNT-based ternary photocatalysts are successfully prepared by a simple anodization and immersion process. The TNT/NiO/CQD ternary nanocomposites produce 1.47 μmol·cm-2·h-1 (
≈
c.a. 2834 μmol·g-1·h-1) of CH4 as the sole product under AM 1.5 illumination. This is five times higher performance than that of bare TNT and the performance is comparable to that of any other TiO2-based unitary, binary, or ternary photocatalysts. This highly enhanced performance results from effective junction formation between TNT and NiO NP, and the dual role of CQDs as a light absorber and charge transporter/collector in the system. The formation of Z-scheme at the TNT/NiO interface leads to efficient charge transfer via a TiO2/NiO bond. The light absorption of the photocatalysts is enhanced and extended by decorated CQDs due to their small band gap. The charge transfer and collection are further improved by charge depletion at the TNT/CQD interface and the charge transfer at the NiO/CQD interface. This work provides a possible model for the realization of efficient and economically affordable photocatalytic CO2 conversion.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Titanium nanotubes
NiO nanoparticles
Carbon quantum dots
Ternary nanocomposites
CO2 conversion

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of nickel (II) hydroxide solution and fabrication of TNT/NiO nanocomposites
Synthesis of CQDs and fabrication of TNT/NiO/CQD
Surface morphologies and material properties of the TNT-based ternary nanocomposites
Photocatalytic CO2 conversion performance by the TNT-based ternary nanocomposites
Optical and electrochemical characterization of the TNT-based ternary photocatalysts
Proposed mechanism for an activated CO2 conversion by the TNT/NiO/CQD ternary photocatalysts

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Surface morphologies of fabricated TNT-based samples with/without the decoration of NiO NPs and CQDs: (a) BT0 (b) NT5 (c) NT16 (d) NT25 (e) NT50 (f) NT16C180.
X-ray diffraction patterns of the various TNT-based photocatalysts with/without NiO NPs and CQDs.
(a) HR-TEM image and (b) magnified images of NT16C180 (c) HAADF-STEM image and elemental mapping of NT16C180 for (d) all elements (e) Ti (f) O, (g) Ni, and (h) C.
XPS spectra of BT0, NT16, and NT16C80: (a) C 1s (b) O 1s (c) Ti 2p.

(a) Production rates of the samples (b) CH4 production rates of NT16C180 under different reaction conditions (c) GC mass analysis of CH4 using 13CO2 for photocatalytic conversion of NT16C180 (d) cyclic performance measurement every 2h.

Optical and electrochemical properties of the various TNT-based photocatalysts: (a) absorbance spectra (b) transient photocurrent measurement (c) PL spectra at the excitation wavelength of 300 nm (d) Nyquist plots of the samples in a CO2-saturated solution measured at 1.5 V of an applied bias.
(a) UPS spectrum of BT0, NT16, and NT16C180 (b) enlarged spectra in higher binding energy area and (c) lower binding energy area. A schematic of (d) TNT/NiO and (e) TNT/NiO/CQD structures.
The proposed structure and mechanism of the TNT/NiO/CQD photocatalyst for CO2 conversion.

head.ce:title: Efficient and economically affordable TiO2 nanotube-based ternary photocatalysts for CO2 conversion boosted by NiO nanoparticles and carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Photonics, National Sun Yat-Sen University, No. 70, Lien-Hai Rd, Kaohsiung 80424, Taiwan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electrical Engineering, The University of Hong Kong, Hong Kong 999077, Hong Kong Special Administrative Region
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bDepartment of Electrical Engineering, The University of Hong Kong, Hong Kong 999077, Hong Kong Special Administrative Region

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute Materials Science and Engineering, National Central University, No. 300 Jhong-da Rd, Jhongli, Taoyuan 320, Taiwan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cInstitute Materials Science and Engineering, National Central University, No. 300 Jhong-da Rd, Jhongli, Taoyuan 320, Taiwan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Photocatalytic CO2 conversion, in general, requires the incorporation of expensive and noble materials to achieve a highly efficient conversion rate. Herein, the TNT/NiO/CQD ternary nanocomposites without any expensive materials and material modification are fabricated for the demonstration of efficient and economically affordable photocatalytic CO2 conversion, to our best knowledge, for the first time. The TNT-based ternary photocatalysts are successfully prepared by a simple anodization and immersion process. The TNT/NiO/CQD ternary nanocomposites produce 1.47 μmol·cm-2·h-1 (
≈
c.a. 2834 μmol·g-1·h-1) of CH4 as the sole product under AM 1.5 illumination. This is five times higher performance than that of bare TNT and the performance is comparable to that of any other TiO2-based unitary, binary, or ternary photocatalysts. This highly enhanced performance results from effective junction formation between TNT and NiO NP, and the dual role of CQDs as a light absorber and charge transporter/collector in the system. The formation of Z-scheme at the TNT/NiO interface leads to efficient charge transfer via a TiO2/NiO bond. The light absorption of the photocatalysts is enhanced and extended by decorated CQDs due to their small band gap. The charge transfer and collection are further improved by charge depletion at the TNT/CQD interface and the charge transfer at the NiO/CQD interface. This work provides a possible model for the realization of efficient and economically affordable photocatalytic CO2 conversion.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: CO2 conversion by photocatalysts is a useful way to reduce CO2 concentration in the atmosphere and exploit it as a valuable fuel source [1]. The process of CO2 conversion by photocatalysts offers a reliable strategy to harness the solar spectrum, reduce fossil fuel dependency, and tackle CO2 emissions associated with energy production by fossil fuels [2]. Titanium dioxide (TiO2) has been taken as a conventional material for photocatalytic CO2 conversion owing to its abundance, chemical & thermal stability, nontoxicity, and low cost since the groundbreaking discovery of its photocatalytic properties in 1972 [3–6]. Since then, TiO2 has been investigated in the field of photoelectrochemical cells for water splitting [7], which suggests its potential to be used as a photocatalyst for CO2 conversion. The first demonstration of the photocatalytic CO2 conversion by TiO2 was done by Inoue et al. in 1979 [8] and lots of effort by researchers has been poured into the enhancement of the performance and the stable operation [9,10].

Fundamentally, the photocatalytic CO2 conversion is activated by the absorption of the photons, the generation of electron-hole (e-h) pairs, and the transfer of the charge carriers to the surface of the photocatalysts for CO2 reduction and water oxidation reactions. However, the performance of the photocatalytic CO2 conversion by TiO2 is hindered, particularly, by its limited utilization of visible light ascribed to its wide bandgap and severe e-h recombination rate that limit the number of available charge carriers at the surface of a photocatalyst for CO2 reduction and water oxidation reactions [9].

In this context, expanding light absorption and enhancing carrier transfer properties in TiO2 are the main factors to improve its photocatalytic performance. To achieve these objectives, several strategies have been studied and employed such as the incorporation of low-dimensional nanostructures, the material modification for TiO2, and the formation of various junctions with metal or nonmetal materials [10,11]. Among those strategies, the formation of low-dimensional nanostructures such as TiO2 nanotubes (TNTs) is one of the attractive ways to improve conversion performance due to enlarged surface area and excellent carrier transport properties [10,11]. Although the formation of TNTs appears to be promising for improving the reaction sites and carrier lifetime in TiO2, the wide bandgap and charge transfer problem at interfaces remains unsettled, still, harnessing only a small fraction of the solar spectrum (⁓5%) inefficiently [12]. For more efficient CO2 conversion by TNT photocatalysts, a typical and immediate solution would apply noble metals such as Pt, Au, and Pd to TNTs as cocatalysts [13–15] but this way has inherent disadvantages in the economical aspect explicitly.

One of the alternative options for this problem is to explore other cheap semiconductor materials such as g-C3N4, ZnO, and NiO to form a junction for efficient charge transfer and collection [16–21]. Among these candidates, NiO is one of the promising materials for the fabrication of efficient and affordable photocatalysts owing to its abundance, low cost, excellent chemical stability, and nontoxicity. In addition, since NiO has p-type conduction and a favorable band structure to that of TiO2, NiO can form an effective pn heterojunction to TiO2 for improved charge transfer characteristics [22–24]. Yet, NiO also possesses its unique wide bandgap of ∼3.6 eV similar to the properties of TiO2 and this makes the utilization of the solar spectrum challenging in a wider range. It is hard to expect a remarkable improvement in optical properties even from the combination with TiO2.

To broaden the available solar spectrum and stay in line with the economic aspect, incorporating carbon quantum dots (CQDs) would be one of the solutions. CQDs are relatively cheap, biocompatible, dispersible, high quantum yield, and photostable [25]. More importantly, CQDs can play an essential role in charge collection and light absorption, combining with TiO2-based photocatalysts due to their favorable energy band structure to that of TiO2 and suitable bandgap [26–28].

In this study, we demonstrate TNT/NiO/CQD nanostructured ternary photocatalysts for efficient and economically affordable CO2 conversion. To our best knowledge, this is the first demonstration of TNT/NiO/CQD nanostructured ternary photocatalysts, which are not equipped with any other expensive materials and have no material modification, for CO2 conversion, The prepared photocatalysts exhibit effective bonds both at TNT/NiO and TNT/CQD interfaces for excellent charge transport characteristics, activating Z-scheme conduction of carriers. In addition, the decorated CQD plays the dual role of light absorber and charge collector, which further improves the performance of CO2 conversion by TNT/NiO/CQD nanostructured ternary photocatalysts. Thus, this study presents a cost-effective ternary photocatalyst accompanied by a simple preparation technique, providing a comparatively high production rate even in comparison to the TiO2-based modified or decorated photocatalysts.

Titanium foil was purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific (USA). Nickel nitrate hexahydrate was obtained from Alfa Aesar Thermo Fisher Scientific (UK). Sodium hydroxide was obtained from Caustic SODA. L-glutamic acid and m-Phenylenediamine flakes were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. All the materials were used without further modification. The solvents used here such as acetone, ethanol, and isopropyl alcohol were of analytical grade.

The titanium foils of 2
×
2 cm2 were sonicated for 15 min in acetone, ethanol, and DI water. The titanium foils were used as an anodic electrode while Pt mesh (20 mm) was used as a cathode in a fluoride-containing electrolyte. The electrolyte was prepared with 400 ml of ethylene glycol, 8 ml of DI water, and 1.2 of NH4F. A bias of 30 V was applied for 2 h for the anodization process, then the anodized foils were rinsed with DI water and subsequently subjected to annealing at 450 °C for 3 h in a muffle furnace. The fabricated samples are named BT0.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nickel (II) hydroxide solution and fabrication of TNT/NiO nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: A 0.5 M of nickel nitrate hexahydrate was dispersed in 100 mL of DI water and stirred for 5 min. The pH of the solution was adjusted by adding NaOH solution (10 mol/L). Then, the mixture was stirred for 5 min to obtain a Ni(OH)2 solution. The Ni(OH)2 solution was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 2 min for washing Ni(OH)2 gel. The washing process was repeated with DI water and ethanol three times each. After the cleaning steps, the obtained Ni(OH)2 gel was dissolved in DI water to make a green Na(OH)2 solution for the decoration process. To form TNT/NiO nanocomposites, the prepared TNT samples were dipped for 5 min into the Ni(OH)2 solutions prepared with various concentrations, stirring the solution at 200 rpm. The various Ni(OH)2 solutions were prepared, dispersing 1000 μ g of the Ni(OH)2 gel in 200, 60, 40, and 20 ml of DI water. The TNT/NiO samples by the concentrations were named NT5, NT16, NT25, and NT50, respectively, after the calcination at 270 °C for 2 h and the formation of NiO NPs on the surfaces of TNTs. A schematic for the sample preparation is briefly described in Fig. S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CQDs and fabrication of TNT/NiO/CQD
ce:section.ce:para: 919.55 mg of L-glutamic acid and 67.575 mg of m-Phenylenediamine flakes were dissolved in 50 mL of DI water, stirring for 1 h. After that, the solution was left aside at room temperature for 1h to obtain supernatant liquid. The supernatant liquid was used for the synthesis of CQDs in a Teflon-lined autoclave at 180 °C for 6 h. After the reaction, the autoclave was cooled down at room temperature and the solution was filtered with a 0.22 μ m PVDF syringe filter. Finally, 1 ml of CQD solution is mixed with 15 ml of DI water for decoration. The multiple TNT/NiO samples were prepared by immersing them in CQDs solvent at 80 °C for 1,2, and 3 h, which were named NT16C60, NT16C120, and NT16C180, respectively. A schematic for the sample preparation is briefly described in Fig. S2.

The crystallinities of the samples were measured at room temperature in the range 20-80 degrees by X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Rigaku Mini flex). The morphology and crystal structure of the samples were investigated by a field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, JEOL JSM-6700F) set to 10 kV, and a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL 2100). The surface of the samples was scraped and mixed with DI water then placed on a C-coated copper grid (TED PELLA TEM GRIDS) to create samples for TEM measurements. FEG-TEM Tecnai F20 G2 was used to capture high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM), selected area electron diffraction (SAED), and high-angle annular dark-field scanning transmission electron microscopy (HAADF-STEM) mapping. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo-scientific instrument) was used to analyze the chemical composition of the samples. The work function was evaluated by UV photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS). The optical properties of samples were investigated by UV-vis spectrometer (Shimadzu, UV2600i) and spectrofluorometer (Horiba, Fluoromax-4). An electrochemical station (CHI660D) was used to carry out the transient photocurrent measurement under AM 1.5 illumination (100 mW/cm2) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurement under dark condition. A gas chromatography-mass spectrometer (GC-MS) was measured with 13CO2 to validate the photocatalyst reaction.

The process of CO2 conversion was investigated via a stainless-steel reactor furnished with a quartz window. The sample was positioned at the bottom of the reactor and evacuated by a mechanical pump. After the evacuation, the reactor was filled up by the injection of CO2 at 25 sccm of flow rate that was controlled by a mass flow controller through a water bubbler to provide CO2/water mixed vapor for the reaction, until ambient pressure was achieved. The evacuation and gas injection processes were repeated three times. The reaction was carried out at normal pressure and room temperature while being illuminated through the quartz window of the reactor under AM 1.5 illumination at 50 % relative humidity. The reaction time was 2 h and the products from the reactor were collected by an airtight syringe. The concentration of the produced species was analyzed with a gas chromatograph (Shimadzu-Nexis GC-2030).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface morphologies and material properties of the TNT-based ternary nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 shows the surface morphologies of the fabricated TNT-based samples by FESEM. All TNT samples exhibit well-aligned and vertically oriented nanotube structures. The undecorated TNTs (BT0 in Figs. 1(a) and S3) exhibit a uniform nanotube array with an average diameter of ∼80 nm, length of ⁓7 µm, and 10-12 nm of wall thicknesses. The NiO-decorated TNT sample prepared by the most diluted Ni(OH)2 solution, NT5 shows no visible difference in its surface morphology in Fig. 1(b) from that of BT0 while other NiO-decorated TNT samples with thicker concentrations of Ni(OH)2 solution (NT16, NT25, and NT50) exhibit a visible sign of denser decoration on the surface of TNT samples in Fig. 1(c)–(e). The NiO decoration onto the surface of TNTs becomes thicker and denser with thicker concentrations of Ni(OH)2 solution, showing aggregation of particles. In the case of NT50, the decorated NiO NPs partially block the entrance of TNTs with severe aggregation of particles. Additional decoration of CQDs to make TNT/NiO nanocomposites further denser due to additional CQDs decorations as shown in Fig. 1(c) and (f).

The crystal structure of the TNT-based ternary samples was studied by measuring XRD. The diffraction patterns are shown in Fig. 2 . From BT0, 2θ values were measured at 25.58°, 37.16°, 38.64°, 53.2°, and 70.82°, which correspond to (101), (103), (112), (202), and (220) crystalline planes, respectively, confirming anatase TiO2 crystal structure (JCPDS-3752 QM:C). With the decoration of NiO NPs and CQDs, there are no secondary peaks or highly remarkable changes found in the XRD patterns. This could result from their low weight percentage ratios and the uniform distribution of the NiO NPs and CQDs [21,26]. One interesting finding from XRD results is that (004) crystal plane is slightly decreased with the continuous deposition of NiO NPs and CQD. Since (004) crystal facet is related to the degree of crystallinity for the growth of TNTs [29,30], it is speculated that the NiO and CQD decoration may affect the crystallinity of TNT, possibly inducing a disorder of crystal structure at its surfaces and the decoration of CQDs appears to be more influential factor than that of NiO due to relative denser decoration of CQDs than NiO. However, the degree of impact is still not significant enough to influence either the change of the crystal structure or the formation of the secondary peak due to the relatively lower loading of the nanoparticles as mentioned above.

To further investigate the properties of various TNT-based photocatalysts, HR-TEM and HAADF-STEM measurements were implemented in Fig. 3 . With 0.35 nm of lattice spacing for TiO2, the grey nanoparticles appearing in the TEM images could be determined to NiO nanoparticles of ∼ 5 nm in diameter and the blackish aggregated particles covering the surface of TNTs are identified as CQDs of ∼2 nm in diameter which are well matched to 0.24 nm and 0.19 nm of lattice spacings, respectively as represented in Fig. 3(b). The distribution of NiO NPs is covered both inside of TNT and the top surface while the decoration of CQDs shows widely scattered distribution in locally aggregated patterns as shown in Figs. 1 and 3(a). The results from the elemental mapping by HAADF-STEM also display these patterns of the distribution for NiO NPs and CQDs on the surface of TNT in Fig. 3(c)–(h). The elemental mapping results show a large amount of Ti and O elements for TNT and uniformly distributed Ni over the surface of TNTs, further proving uniform decoration of NiO NPs over the surface of TNT. Although C element distribution appears to be dense over the entire surface of TNT, possibly including background carbon elements, the density of the C elements is obviously thick in the place where CQDs are located, implying those C elements resulted from the successfully decorated CQDs. In addition, the presence of CQD in NT16C180 is identified with its signature peaks at 1362 and 1571 cm-1 by Raman spectroscopy in Fig. S4. The SAED pattern also supports the presence of all the elements for TNTs, NiO NPs, and CQDs in Fig. S5, implying well-crystallized TNT, NiO NP, and CQD materials.

The elemental composition and chemical state of the surface of the samples were investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The survey spectra represent the presence of O 1s, Ti 2p, C 1s, and Ni 2p for BT0, NT16, and NT16C180 from our samples in Fig. S6(a) [31,32]. This XPS survey spectra also prove the decoration of NiO NPs for NT16 and NT16C180, corresponding with the results from SEM and TEM. C elements are detected in common for all the samples because, in general, C elements can be measured because of adventitious carbon residues from the surrounding atmosphere. To implement a delicate study on the impact of NiO and CQDs on our samples, high-resolution XPS measurement was carried out in Figs. 4 and S6(b). Peak fitting to the spectra was selected using Gaussian-Lorentzian peak shape after subtraction of Shirley background as shown in Fig. 4. Fig. 4(a) shows high-resolution C 1s spectra. For all samples, the characteristic peaks from the C 1s spectra were found at 284.7 and 286.3 eV, which are assigned to C-C/C=C and C-O, respectively [26,27,33]. Just as in the case of the survey spectrum, no clear distinction can be made for the presence of CQDs from adventitious carbon elements. For NT16C180, one more distinctive peak is observed at 288 eV for C=O and this proves the successful decoration on the surfaces of TNT/NiO, suggesting the formation of effective bonds between CQDs and the oxide semiconductor materials such as Ti-O-C [34,35]. The high-resolution O 1s spectra show two prominent peaks at 529.8 and 531 eV that are assigned to the lattice oxygen of TiO2 and Ti-OH, respectively, suggesting the TNT contains a typical TiO2 material with oxygen vacancies in Fig. 4(b) [36]. NT16 samples show a broad shape of the O 1s spectrum due to the presence of lattice oxygen for NiO and Ni-OH at 530.2 and 531.5 eV [32]. This result is similar to the report from others and signifies the decoration of NiO NPs on the surface of TNT again [32]. The high-resolution O 1s spectrum of NT16C180 includes two additional peaks at 531.8 and 532.4 eV for C=O and C-O, respectively [26,27,33]. Again, that provides evidence for the presence of CQDs in the samples. The high-resolution Ti 2p spectrum of BT0 exhibits two prominent peaks at 464.3 eV and 458.6 eV, which are associated with Ti 2p1/2 and Ti 2p3/2, respectively and the energy separation between these peaks is ⁓5.7 eV [37]. With the decoration of NiO NPs to the surface of TNT, a secondary peak is well-developed at 457.3 eV, which is a signature peak for the presence of Ti3+ [32] and the peak is related to the formation of the bond between TiO2 and NiO [38]. Even after the decoration of CQD, the Ti3+-related peak still exists, implying a strong bonding between TNT and NiO NPs remains intact. Another remarkable finding from the Ti 2p spectrum is that the decorations of NiO NP and CQDs induce a shift to lower binding energy. The shift to lower binding energy is related to the charge transfer at interfaces via the established bond [39,40]. In other words, TNT experiences electron accumulations by the decoration of NiO NPs and CQD, forming an effective junction at those interfaces.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic CO2 conversion performance by the TNT-based ternary nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para:
The CO2 conversion performance was evaluated for BT0, NT5, NT16, NT25, NT50, NT16C60, NT16C120, and NT16C180 under AM 1.5 illumination as shown in Fig. 5 (a). All the samples yielded methane without any other detectable species, showing the excellent selective conversion of CO2. The conversion performance of the TNT photocatalysts improves with the decoration of NiO NPs and NT16 showed a three times larger performance than that of BT0 (0.27 μmol·cm-2·h-1 for BT0 → 0.81 μmol·cm-2·h-1 for NT16). The enhancement in the CH4 production rate with NiO NP decoration is dependent on Ni(OH)2 solution concentration but the heavier loading of NiO NPs deteriorates the conversion performance (NT25 and NT50), probably due to massive loss of reaction sites as shown in Fig. 1. Additional decoration of CQDs was beneficial for further improvement of the conversion performance. With the decoration of CQDs on the surface of the NT16 samples, the CH4 production rate reaches 1.47 μmol·cm-2·h-1 (
≈
c.a. 2834 μmol·g-1·h-1) for NT16C180. This is five times higher performance than that of bare TNT, and the performance is comparable to that of any other TiO2-based unitary, binary, or ternary photocatalysts as listed in Table S2 in the supporting information. To our best knowledge, this is the first demonstration of CO2 conversion by the TNT/NiO/CQD ternary photocatalysts. Moreover, despite the additional decoration of CQDs, CH4 is the main species still for CO2 conversion by our catalysts.

To verify the performance measurement reliability, several control tests were performed under various reaction conditions such as without a photocatalyst, without irradiation, substituting Ar for CO2, and without H2O injection as shown in Fig. 5(b). All results from the control tests validate the reliability of the measurement further, excluding the error from possible contamination and impurities. Another measurement for further reliability of the results was conducted, using 13CO2 gas together with GC-MS as shown in Fig. 5(c). Combined with the results from Fig. 5(b), the detection of the signal at m/z = 17 implies that the detected CH4 originated from the injected CO2 and the results via the photocatalytic reaction activated by our photocatalysts. Fig. 5(d) represents the results of cyclic performance evaluation every 2h, which proves the stability of our catalyst for its continuous operation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical and electrochemical characterization of the TNT-based ternary photocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 6 shows the optical and electrochemical properties of the various TNT-based photocatalysts that were measured to understand the origin of their photocatalytic performances. To determine the light absorption properties of the samples, the UV-vis diffuse reflectance spectra were measured in the range of 300-800 nm wavelengths, as shown in Fig. 6(a). BT0 exhibits higher absorbance in the UV region with wavelengths below 390 nm, which could be attributed to the ⁓3.2 eV bandgap of anatase TiO2 [41]. BT0 also shows an additional absorption at ∼ 600 nm that could be attributed to the dispersion of light caused by the cracks and pores in the nanotube arrays or the presence of the oxygen vacancies found in Fig. 4 [36,42]. The deposition of NiO NPs on the surfaces of TNTs is not successful in making a substantial improvement in the absorption properties showing a relatively smaller improvement in the absorption at ∼500 nm. These would be the results of the relatively low and uniform loading of NiO NPs in the TNT/NiO sample as shown in Fig. 3 and its wider bandgap (3.54 eV) of NiO materials than that of TNT (3.18 eV) in Fig. S7. A more remarkable improvement is made with the decoration of CQD in Fig. 6(a). The absorbance at wavelengths ranging from 400 nm to 600 nm is highly enhanced, proving the visible light absorption capability of CQDs decorated in the TNT/NiO system. In general, CQDs possess their featured absorption peak at ∼350 nm that is attributed to the n-π* transition of the C=O bond, and depending on their morphologies such as size or shape, the absorbance spectrum can be adjustable [28,33,43]. Our synthesized CQDs show a relatively smaller bandgap (2.45 eV in Fig. S7) and the narrow bandgap of the CQDs allows more widened and efficient absorbance in the visible light region for the catalytic system. The CQDs play an important role as an efficient light absorber in our photocatalytic system.

Fig. 6(b) shows the results of transient photocurrent measurements. BT0 and NT5 have almost the same level of photocurrent which is attributed to the low loading of NiO NPs for NT5 as shown in Fig. 1(b). NT 16, NT25, and NT50 represent the small improvement in photocurrents probably, owing to the effect of relatively dense NiO NPs on the surface of TNTs but no remarkable improvement can be found with further dense decoration. Except for the notably lower photoresponse of NT5, almost no meaningful distinction can be measured even with further decoration of NiO NPs. This is because optical absorption is limited by its wide bandgap of 3.54 eV in Fig. S6. More remarkable differences in the photocurrent measurement were found with the CQD decoration as in Fig. 6(a). The photocurrent of NT16C60 is remarkably increased with the CQD decoration in comparison with that of BT0 and NT5, corresponding to the results from its absorption properties in Fig. 1(a). Further enhanced photocurrents were observed with increased decoration time, owing to more available electron-hole pairs by enlarged light absorption in Fig. 6(a).

Fig. 6(c) displays the results from PL measurements. The emission peak is detected at ∼ 390 nm of wavelength from BT0, which is well-matched to the bandgap of TiO2 [44]. The NiO NP decoration onto the surface of the TNTs enhances the quenching of the PL intensity and, especially, NT16 exhibits the most efficient quenching, implying the most efficient charge transfer from TNT to NiO NPs at the TNT/NiO interface. The varied degrees of quenching appeared to be affected by the distribution and amount of NiO NPs as shown in Figs. 1 and 3. The CQD decoration activates further improvement in quenching, possibly supposing further improved charge transfer from TNT to NiO NPs and, probably to CQDs. It is speculated that this improved charge transfer at the TNT/NiO interface also affects the results of the transient photocurrent together with small additional absorption by NiO NPs in the comparison between BT0 and NiO NP-decorated samples in Fig. 6(b).

EIS measurements were implemented to the study charge transfer of the various TNT-based photocatalysts at the interface of the photocatalyst/CO2 electrolyte as shown in Fig. 6(d). The diameters of the semicircles are related to charge transfer resistance at the photocatalyst/CO2 electrolyte interface and these are used for indicators of charge transfer capability at the interface [45,46]. All the decorated samples with NiO NPs showed smaller diameters, implying enhanced charge transfer by the decoration of the NiO NPs at the photocatalyst/CO2 electrolyte interface. NT16 shows the most superior charge transfer capability while NT25 and NT50 are not favorable in obtaining further improvement, possibly owing to highly excessive and nonuniform loading as shown in Fig. 1. Furthermore, the presence of CQDs leads to a further decrease in the diameter of the semicircles in Fig. 6(d), which means a decrease in the charge transfer resistance more. It appears that the properties at the TNT/NiO and TNT/NiO/CQD interfaces in Fig. 6(c) contribute to the enhancement of the properties at the photocatalyst/CO2 electrolyte interface.

For a deeper understanding of the band structure of the TNT-based photocatalysts, UPS measurements were carried out in Fig. 7 for BT0, NT16, and NT16C180. The work functions, ϕ of these samples were extracted from the equation, ϕ = hν − (Ecut-off – EF) where ϕ , hν, Ecut-off, and EF represent the work function (eV), excitation energy (here, 21.22 eV for He I), secondary cut-off energy (eV), and Fermi level (eV), respectively [36,47]. The extracted work functions from Fig. 7 are 4.78, 4.32, and 4.83 eV for BT0, NT16, and NT16C180, respectively. The results explain that the NiO decoration modifies the work function to a smaller number at the TNT/NiO interface while the CQD decoration modifies the work function to a larger number. In other words, the decoration of NiO NPs over the surface of TNTs enables the electrons of TNT at the surface more concentrated. Together with measured valence band postions for TNT (2.94 eV), NiO (0.8 eV), and CQD (2.45 eV) by valence band x-ray spectroscopy in Fig. S8 and optical bandgaps for TNT (3.18 eV), NiO (3.54 eV), and CQD (2.45 eV) in Fig. S7, this can construct a Z-scheme pathway for charge transfer at the TNT/NiO interface as depicted in 7(d) [48–50]. This is well-corresponded to the electron accumulation found in Fig. 4 and the direction of photogenerated charge transfer in Fig. 6(c). The CQD decoration over the TNT/NiO samples would form two possible interfaces, TNT/CQD and NiO/CQD. However, the results from the UPS measurement probably could not contain the properties of TNT/NiO interface, considering a shallow ∼2.5 nm UPS probing depth and the location of the TNT/NiO interface underneath densely deposited CQDs and NiO NPs with ∼2 nm and ∼5 nm diameters, respectively, differently from the XPS results in Fig. 4. In other words, the increased work function of the TNT/NiO/CQD sample can be interpreted from the properties of TNT/CQD and NiO/CQD interfaces. Although the difference in the work function is a quite small number of 0.05 eV, the TNT/CQD interface can deplete the electrons in TNT and make TNT less n-type, allowing an enlarged work function difference between TNT and NiO NPs. In turn, the larger difference in work functions establishes a stronger internal electric field at the TNT/NiO interface for enhanced charge transfer via the established Z-scheme pathway [51]. In addition, even though the effect from the TNT/CQD interface would be more significant than that from the NiO/CQD because more surface area of TNT than that of NiO NPs for CQD decoration is available in Fig. 3, the NiO/CQD interface can contribute to the enhancement of CO2 conversion performance. Owing to the conduction band edge position of CQD, the electrons in the conduction band of NiO can be easily transferred to that of CQDs as shown in Fig. S9. The further charge accumulation for the formation of the stronger internal electric field at the TNT/NiO interface and the resulting direction of photogenerated charge flow are further matched with the results from XPS, PL, and EIS in Figs. 4 and 6.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proposed mechanism for an activated CO2 conversion by the TNT/NiO/CQD ternary photocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 8 describes the proposed mechanism of the CO2 conversion by the TNT/NiO/CQD photocatalyst. Basically, the TNT/NiO interface forms an internal electrical field that activates Z-scheme carrier conduction, which allows electron and hole accumulation on the conduction band (CB) of NiO and the valence band (VB) of TNT. This Z-scheme structure can be strengthened by the decoration of CQD, depleting the electrons of TNT more. Simultaneously, the decoration of CQDs helps not only to enable additional light absorption that extends to the visible light region further but also charge collector/transporter. This greater availability of charge carrier concentrations and directionality of charge flow boost more efficient CO2 reduction and water oxidation reactions on the surfaces of CQDs and TNTs, respectively. These highly improved CO2 reduction and water oxidation reactions of our ternary TNT-based ternary photocatalysts were further confirmed electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) measurement as shown in Fig. S10, and this leads to enhanced performance of the CO2 conversion by the TNT/NiO/CQD photocatalyst.

In conclusion, the photocatalytic CO2 conversion was successfully demonstrated by the efficient and economically affordable TNT/NiO/CQD ternary nanocomposites fabricated by facile anodization and immersion process, without using any expensive materials and material modifications, for the first time. Under AM 1.5 illumination, the TNT/NiO/CQD ternary nanocomposites produced 1.47 μmol·cm-2·h-1 (
≈
c.a. 2834 μmol·g-1·h-1) of CH4 as the sole product from CO2 conversion reaction. This is five times higher performance than that of bare TNT, and the performance is comparable to that of any other TiO2-based unitary, binary, or ternary photocatalysts. This highly enhanced performance was attributed to effective junction formation between TNT and NiO NP, and the dual role of CQDs in the system. First, charge transfer was improved by the formation of a Z-scheme at the TNT/NiO interface via a TiO2/NiO bond. Second, light absorption was enhanced and extended by a smaller band gap of CQDs. Third, charge transfer and collection were further improved by charge depletion at the TNT/CQD interface and the charge transfer at NiO/CQD interface. This work provides a possible model for the realization of efficient and economically affordable photocatalytic CO2 conversion.

Tarek Fawzi: Writing – original draft, Data curation, Methodology, Investigation. Pei-Chen Huang: Data curation, Investigation. Jinwook Kim: Investigation. Wei-Hsuan Hung: Writing – review & editing. Wei-Chun Lin: Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Supervision. Hyeonseok Lee: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support of the National Science and Technology Council of Taiwan, grant no. NSTC 111-2222-E-110-007-MY2.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.electacta.2024.144587.
